skill.name: SKILL: judgment_hygiene
skill.description: Internal structural hygiene for judgment-bearing outputs. Enforces separation of observation, inference, evaluation, and recommendation with proper grounding and abstention protocols.
skill.summary: Ensures clean epistemic structure in judgment-bearing outputs through observation/inference separation, certainty discipline, evaluation grounding, and honest abstention.
skill.author: SyntagmaNull

security_audit.summary: Prompt-only skill with no executable code. The skill file contains detailed structural guidelines for epistemic hygiene in AI responses. No security patterns detected. The content focuses on improving reasoning structure through observation/inference separation, certainty discipline, evaluation grounding, and honest abstention protocols. Safe for publication.

content.user_title: Improve reasoning structure and epistemic hygiene
content.value_statement: Prevents common reasoning failures where AI models present inference as observation or give costless recommendations. This skill enforces structural discipline through six systematic checks that ensure claims are properly grounded and uncertainty is honestly represented.
actual_capabilities: Separates observation from inference in multimodal inputs (text, images, audio)
Enforces certainty discipline with differential confidence based on grounding strength
Grounds all evaluations in observable or inferred evidence rather than floating judgments
Attaches specific tradeoffs to nontrivial recommendations including cost, risk, and reversibility
Implements honest abstention modes (full, partial, conditional, information-seeking)
Resists frame effects by distinguishing irrelevant narrative drift from relevant structural changes
limitations: Does not guarantee factual truth or correct world knowledge
Does not eliminate all forms of bias or mimicry in AI responses
Requires models to self-monitor adherence which may fail under pressure
Focused on structural hygiene only and does not address content accuracy or moral correctness

use_cases.title: AI-assisted research analysis
use_cases.description: When conducting research interviews where the model must distinguish between what participants directly said versus interpretations of their intent or meaning.
use_cases.target_user: Researchers and academics analyzing qualitative data

use_cases.title: High-stakes decision support
use_cases.description: When making business, medical, or technical recommendations where the cost of action and burden of tradeoffs must be explicitly stated.

use_cases.title: Multimodal content moderation
use_cases.description: When evaluating images, videos, or text where emotional state, intent, or cause must be inferred from observable features without overstating certainty.

prompt_templates.title: Basic epistemic check
prompt_templates.prompt: Use judgment_hygiene skill. Analyze this input and tell me what can be directly observed versus what must be inferred. Distinguish clearly between facts and interpretations.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick analysis of documents, images, or statements to separate direct evidence from interpretation

prompt_templates.title: Decision support with tradeoffs
prompt_templates.prompt: Use judgment_hygiene skill. Evaluate this decision problem. For each recommendation, explicitly state what it would cost in time, money, risk, reversibility, and opportunity cost. Mark what you don't know and can't judge.
prompt_templates.scenario: Making significant decisions where cost-benefit analysis requires transparent grounding

prompt_templates.title: Uncertainty quantification
prompt_templates.prompt: Use judgment_hygiene skill. Assess this situation. Show differential confidence: what are you highly certain about versus what is speculative? Ground each confidence level in specific evidence. If grounds are insufficient, use abstention mode.
prompt_templates.scenario: Analysis under uncertainty where distinguishing strong from weak conclusions is critical

prompt_templates.title: Frame-resistant analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Use judgment_hygiene skill. This topic may be emotionally or politically charged. Resist narrative framing and focus on structural dependencies. Would your judgment change if the wording changed but the facts stayed the same? If not, the structure is sound.
prompt_templates.scenario: Analyzing controversial topics without being swayed by emotional or political language

output_examples.input: Is the person in this photo angry?
output_examples.output: I can see furrowed brows, a tight jaw, and compressed lips. These are directly observable features. Anger is one plausible interpretation, but the expression alone doesn't fix a single emotion - frustration, concentration, or determination could also fit. Without more context, I can't confidently label a specific emotion.

Switching would solve your scaling issues, but here's the cost: team needs 2-3 months to learn Kubernetes, you'll rewrite deployment pipelines, existing monitoring becomes partially invalid, and you can't easily revert. If your scaling problem is urgent and you have the learning budget, it makes sense. If you need a quick fix or your team is already stretched, a simpler solution might be better.

output_examples.input: What's causing the bug in my code?
output_examples.output: I can tell you what's definitely wrong based on the error message: line 42 is trying to access a property on undefined. That's observation. What I can't determine without seeing more code is why that value is undefined - it could be the API response structure changed, or the variable isn't being initialized, or there's a race condition. Could you show me the data fetching code?

best_practices: Use the six structural checks silently in your reasoning rather than outputting explicit role labels unless the user specifically requests the reasoning structure to be visible
Apply the meta-rule continuously - ask whether you're changing the actual dependency structure or just adding reasoning-flavored language to look more careful
Prefer partial or conditional abstention over blanket 'I don't know' when some parts of the question are answerable and others are not
anti_patterns: Adding explicit OBS/INF/EVAL labels to make output look structured without actually changing the underlying dependencies
Using generic phrases like 'this is complex' or 'it depends' to avoid making difficult judgments when judgment is actually possible
Manufacturing weak inferences solely to avoid abstention - for example, saying 'based on general patterns' to fake grounding for an evaluation

faq.question: Do I need to output OBS/INF/EVAL labels for every response?
faq.answer: No. The skill specifies that labels should only be used when they materially improve correctness, honesty, or clarity. Most responses should use the internal checks silently without exposing the structure explicitly.

faq.question: What's the difference between observation and inference for images?
faq.answer: Observation includes directly perceivable features like shapes, colors, spatial arrangements, and motions. Inference includes meaning, intention, emotion, or cause. For example, 'red round object' is observation but 'apple' is conservative inference requiring category recognition.

faq.question: When should I abstain versus give a partial answer?
faq.answer: Use partial abstention when some parts of the question are answerable with available evidence while others are not. Use full abstention only when there is genuinely no basis to judge any part of the question.

faq.question: Does this skill guarantee my AI responses will be correct?
faq.answer: No. This skill only improves structural hygiene - the relationship between observations, inferences, and evaluations. It does not guarantee factual truth, good world knowledge, or perfect reasoning.

faq.question: What's the meta-rule and why does it matter?
faq.answer: The meta-rule checks whether you're actually changing the dependency structure or just adding reasoning-flavored decoration. It prevents 'performing' the skill by adding labels and qualifiers without making the answer more grounded or honest.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for non-judgment tasks like code formatting?
faq.answer: No. This skill is designed for tasks requiring judgment - determining what is true, likely, unclear, or unsupported. Pure formatting, retrieval, or transformation tasks don't benefit from these structural checks.
